(1) *al-Sinwan*: two palm trees that emerge from a single root.
(2) With its fixity in the rank of its causes, not in the rank of itself. "Author's note."

His saying, the Exalted: **قَالَ أَرَأَيْتَكَ هَذَا الَّذِي كَرَّمْتَ عَلَيَّ لَئِنْ أَخَّرْتَنِ إِلَى يَوْمِ الْقِيَامَةِ لَأَحْتَنِكَنَّ ذُرِّيَّتَهُ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا** "He said, ‘Tell me, this one whom You have honoured above me, if You reprieve me until the Day of Resurrection, I will surely destroy his progeny, all but a few.’" (17:62). The *kāf* in the word *ara’aytaka* (أَرَأَيْتَكَ) is augmentative (زائدة) and has no grammatical position; rather, it serves to denote address (الخطاب), as is the case with demonstrative pronouns. What is meant by His saying: "this one whom You have honoured above me" (هذَا الَّذِي كَرَّمْتَ عَلَيَ) is Adam (peace be upon him). His being honored over Iblis refers to his being preferred over him by the command to prostrate to him, and the subsequent banishment of Iblis when he refused.

From this, it becomes apparent that Iblis understood this preference from the command of prostration, just as he ventured to intend the seduction of Adam's progeny based on what transpired in the dialogue between the Exalted and the angels, specifically their saying: **أَتَجْعَلُ فِيهَا مَنْ يُفْسِدُ فِيهَا وَيَسْفِكُ الدِّمَاءَ** "Will You set in it someone who will cause corruption in it and shed blood?" (2:30). What was previously mentioned in the commentary (تفسير) of that verse is beneficial here.

The term *iḥtināk* (الاحتناك), according to *al-Majma‘*, means uprooting or severing from the source (الاقتطاع من الأصل). It is said: "So-and-so *iḥtanaka* what another possessed of wealth or knowledge" when he exhausts it and takes it all. Similarly, "the locusts *iḥtanaka* the crop" when they consume it entirely. It is also said to be derived from their saying: "He *ḥanaka* the beast with its rope" when he places a rope in its lower jaw (حنكها الأسفل) to lead it. It appears that this latter meaning is the root of the matter; thus, *iḥtināk* means bridling (الإلجام).

The meaning is: Iblis said, after he had disobeyed and the Divine wrath had seized him: "O Lord, tell me about this one whom You have preferred over me by commanding me to prostrate to him and banishing me for my disobedience; I swear that if You reprieve me until the Day of Resurrection—which is the duration of the stay of the children of Adam on earth—I will surely bridle his progeny (لألجمن ذريته) except for a few of them," and these are the sincere ones (المخلصون).

His saying, the Exalted: **قَالَ اذْهَبْ فَمَنْ تَبِعَكَ مِنْهُمْ فَإِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ جَزَاؤُكُمْ جَزَاءً مَوْفُورًا** "He said, ‘Go! Whoever of them follows you, then hell shall be the recompense of you [all], a full recompense." (17:63). It has been said that the command to "Go" (اذْهَبْ) is not intended literally, but is rather a metaphor (كناية) for leaving him to his own devices, just as you might say to someone who opposes you: "Do as you wish." Others say the command is literal, being another expression of His saying in another place: **فَاخْرُجْ مِنْهَا فَإِنَّكَ رَجِيمٌ** "Begone from here, for you are indeed an outcast." (15:34). The term *mawfür* (الموفور) means that which is completed; thus, a "full recompense" (الجزاء الموفور) is the reward that is paid in full without anything being withheld. The meaning of the verse is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَاسْتَفْزِزْ مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتَ مِنْهُمْ بِصَوْتِكَ وَأَجْلِبْ عَلَيْهِمْ بِخَيْلِكَ وَرَجِلِكَ** "And incite whomever of them you can with your voice, and rally against them your cavalry and your infantry..." (17:64) until the end of the verse. *Istifzāz* (الاستفزاز) means to unsettle and rouse with lightness and haste. *Ijlāb* (الإجلاب), as stated in *al-Majma‘*, means to drive with a clamor (جلبة) from the driver, and *jalbah* is the intensity of sound. In *al-Mufradāt*, it is said: "The root of *jalb* is the driving of a thing... and *ajlabtu ‘alayhi* means I shouted at him with subjugation."

The term *khayl* (الخيل)—as has been said—literally refers to horses and has no singular form from its own root, and it is used metaphorically for horsemen (الفرسان). The term *rajil* (الرجل) refers to the foot-soldier (الراجل), which is the opposite of the rider. Its being contrasted with "cavalry" (الخيل) suggests that it refers to the infantry (الرجالة), who are the members of the army other than the horsemen.

Thus, His saying: "And incite whomever of them you can with your voice" (وَاسْتَفْزِزْ مَنِ اسْتَطَعْتَ مِنْهُمْ بِصَوْتِكَ) means: rouse toward disobedience whomever you are able to rouse from the progeny of Adam—those who take him as a guardian and follow him, as mentioned in Surah al-Hijr—with your voice. It is as if inciting with the voice is a metaphor for belittling them through false whispering (الوسوسة) that lacks reality, and a representation (تمثيل) of how sheep and other animals are driven by shouting and scolding, which is a sound devoid of [rational] meaning.

His saying: "and rally against them your cavalry and your infantry" (وَأَجْلِبْ عَلَيْهِمْ بِخَيْلِكَ وَرَجِلِكَ) means: shout at them to drive them toward the disobedience of Allah with your assistants and your armies, both their horsemen and their foot-soldiers. This seems to be an indication that among his tribe and assistants are those who perform their actions with speed, as is the nature of cavalry in the heat of battle, and those who are employed in situations other than rapid charges, like the infantry. Thus, "cavalry and infantry" is a metaphor for those who are swift in action and those who are slow, and it contains a representation of the nature of their work.

His saying: **وَشَارِكْهُمْ فِي الْأَمْوَالِ وَالْأَوْلَادِ** "and share with them in wealth and children" (17:64). Partnership (الشركة) can only be conceived in ownership (الملك) and exclusive right (الاختصاص), the consequence of which is that the partner becomes a co-sharer with his associate in the benefit, which is the purpose of acquiring wealth and children. For wealth is an external entity separate from the human being, and likewise, a child is an independent human person separate from his parents. Were it not for the purpose of benefit, man would not consider wealth as property nor a child as an exclusive relation.

Therefore, the Devil's partnership with man in his wealth or child is his participation in the exclusive right and benefit. For instance, wealth—which Allah made to fulfill man's natural needs—might be acquired through unlawful means, so the Devil benefits from it for his purpose and man for his natural purpose. Or he might acquire it through lawful means but use it in other than the obedience of Allah, so they both benefit from it while he remains empty-handed of the mercy of Allah. Similarly, a child might be born through unlawful means, or born lawfully but then raised with an unrighteous upbringing and disciplined with other than the discipline of Allah, thereby giving the Devil a share and himself a share, and so on.

This is a sound perspective on the meaning of the verse and encompasses the various views mentioned by the commentators (المفسرون). For example, some said that the wealth and children in which the Devil shares are every piece of wealth acquired from the forbidden (حرام) or taken unjustly, and every child of adultery, as narrated from Ibn Abbas and others.

Another view is that his partnership in wealth consists of his commanding them to designate animals as *sā’ibah* and *baḥīrah* and the like, and in children by their making them Jews, Christians, or Magians, as narrated from Qatadah.

Another view is that every forbidden wealth and forbidden intimacy involves his partnership, as narrated from al-Kalbi. Another view is that what is meant by children is naming them ‘Abd al-Shams, ‘Abd al-Harith, and the like. Another view is that it refers to the killing of the infant girls (الموءودة) among their children, as also narrated from Ibn Abbas. Another view is that partnership in wealth refers to sacrificing for idols, as narrated from al-Dahhak, among other reports from the early commentators.

His saying: **وَعِدْهُمْ وَمَا يَعِدُهُمُ الشَّيْطَانُ إِلَّا غُرُورًا** "and make promises to them.’ But Satan makes them no promises except to delude [them]." (17:64). That is, he promises them nothing but a deceptive promise by presenting error in the form of correctness and falsehood in the guise of truth. Thus, "delusion" (الغرور) is a verbal noun (مصدر) used in the sense of an active participle for emphasis.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ عِبَادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَانٌ وَكَفَى بِرَبِّكَ وَكِيلًا** "As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them,’ and your Lord suffices as a guardian." (17:65). What is meant by "My servants" (عبادي) is broader than the sincere ones (المخلصون) whom Iblis excluded by his saying: "all but a few" (إِلَّا قَلِيلًا). Rather, it refers to those other than the perverse (الغاوين) among the followers of Iblis, as He said in another place: **إِنَّ عِبَادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطَانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغَاوِينَ** "As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you." (15:42). The possessive construction (الإضافة) here is for honor (التشريف).

His saying: "and your Lord suffices as a guardian" (وَكَفَى بِرَبِّكَ وَكِيلًا) means: as One who watches over their souls and their deeds, preserving their interests and taking charge of their affairs. For the guardian (الوكيل) is the one who sponsors the affairs of another and stands in his place in managing and directing them. By this, it becomes clear that what is meant is His specific guardianship (وكالته الخاصة) for those of His servants who are not perverse, as previously mentioned in Surah al-Hijr.

Various discussions regarding the story of Adam's prostration, which are useful in this context, have already been presented in scattered places of His speech, such as Surah al-Baqarah, Surah al-A‘raf, and Surah al-Hijr.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi*, narrated from Ibn Sinan from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: **وَإِنْ مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ إِلَّا نَحْنُ مُهْلِكُوهَا قَبْلَ يَوْمِ الْقِيَامَةِ** "There is no town but We shall destroy it before the Day of Resurrection" (17:58), he said: "It is annihilation through death or otherwise." In another narration from him (peace be upon him) regarding the same verse, he said: "By killing and death or otherwise."

**I say:** This is perhaps an explanation of the entire verse.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا مَنَعَنَا أَنْ نُرْسِلَ بِالْآيَاتِ** "Nothing kept Us from sending the signs..." (17:59), he said: "It was revealed concerning the Quraysh." He said: In a narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) regarding the verse: "That was because Muhammad asked his people to bring them [signs]—so Gabriel descended and said: 'Indeed Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, says: **وَمَا مَنَعَنَا أَنْ نُرْسِلَ بِالْآيَاتِ إِلَّا أَنْ كَذَّبَ بِهَا الْأَوَّلُونَ** (Nothing kept Us from sending the signs except that the former peoples denied them). Whenever We sent a sign to the Quraysh and they did not believe in it, We would have destroyed them—therefore We delayed the signs from your people.'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad, al-Nasa’i, al-Bazzar, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Tabarani, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), Ibn Mardawayh, al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala’il*, and al-Diya’ in *al-Mukhtarah* narrated from Ibn Abbas who said: "The people of Mecca asked the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to turn al-Safa into gold for them and to remove the mountains from them so they could farm. It was said to him: 'If you wish, We shall be patient with them, or if you wish, We shall give them what they asked—but if they then disbelieve, they shall be destroyed as the nations before them were destroyed.' He said: 'No, rather I will be patient with them.' So Allah revealed: **وَمَا مَنَعَنَا أَنْ نُرْسِلَ بِالْآيَاتِ إِلَّا أَنْ كَذَّبَ بِهَا الْأَوَّلُونَ**."

**I say:** Something similar has been narrated through numerous chains.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir narrated from Sahl ibn Sa‘d who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saw the children of so-and-so leaping upon his pulpit like the leaping of monkeys, and that distressed him. He was never seen laughing fully until he died. So Allah revealed: **وَمَا جَعَلْنَا الرُّؤْيَا الَّتِي أَرَيْنَاكَ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِلنَّاسِ** 'We did not appoint the vision that We showed you except as a tribulation for the people' (17:60)."

In the same source, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ibn ‘Umar that the Prophet said: "I saw the children of al-Hakam ibn Abi al-‘As on the pulpits as if they were monkeys, and Allah revealed concerning that: **وَمَا جَعَلْنَا الرُّؤْيَا الَّتِي أَرَيْنَاكَ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِلنَّاسِ وَالشَّجَرَةَ الْمَلْعُونَةَ** 'We did not appoint the vision that We showed you except as a tribulation for the people and the cursed tree' (17:60), meaning al-Hakam and his children."

In the same source, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ya‘la ibn Murrah who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'I was shown the Umayyads on the pulpits of the earth, and they will soon rule over you, and you will find them to be evil masters.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was grieved by that, so Allah revealed: **وَمَا جَعَلْنَا الرُّؤْيَا الَّتِي أَرَيْنَاكَ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِلنَّاسِ**."

In the same source, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from al-Husayn ibn ‘Ali: "The Messenger of Allah woke up one morning feeling distressed. It was said: 'What is the matter with you, O Messenger of Allah?' He said: 'I was shown in a dream as if the Umayyads were taking turns on this pulpit of mine.' It was said: 'O Messenger of Allah, do not be distressed, for it is only the world (دنيا) that they shall attain.' So Allah revealed: **وَمَا جَعَلْنَا الرُّؤْيَا الَّتِي أَرَيْنَاكَ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِلنَّاسِ**."

In the same source, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala’il*, and Ibn ‘Asakir narrated from Sa‘id ibn al-Musayyib who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saw the Umayyads on the pulpits and that distressed him. Then Allah revealed to him that it is only the world (دنيا) they have been given, so his eyes were comforted. This is His saying: **وَمَا جَعَلْنَا الرُّؤْيَا الَّتِي أَرَيْنَاكَ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِلنَّاسِ**, meaning a trial (بلاء) for the people."

**I say:** It is also narrated in *Tafsir al-Burhan* from al-Tha‘labi in his *Tafsir*, tracing it back to Sa‘id ibn al-Musayyib.

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from the book *Fadilat al-Husayn*, tracing it back to Abu Hurayrah who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'I saw in a dream the children of al-Hakam or the children of al-‘As leaping upon my pulpit as monkeys leap.' He woke up as if enraged, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was never seen laughing fully after that until he died."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Mardawayh narrated from ‘A’ishah that she said to Marwan ibn al-Hakam: "I heard the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saying to your father and your grandfather: 'Indeed, you are the cursed tree in the Qur'an.'"

In *Majma‘ al-Bayan*: "A vision the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) saw that monkeys were ascending his pulpit and descending, and that distressed and grieved him." Narrated by Sahl ibn Sa‘d from his father. Then he said: "And it is what is narrated from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon them), and they said: 'According to this interpretation (تأويل), the cursed tree in the Qur'an is the Umayyads.'"

**I say:** This is not a matter of interpretation (تأويل) at all, but rather it is the actual revelation (تنزيل) as previously explained, except that the term *ta’wīl* was sometimes used in their speech for any explanation of the intended meaning.

Al-‘Ayyashi narrated this meaning in his *Tafsir* from several trustworthy narrators such as Zurarah, Humran, Muhammad ibn Muslim, Ma‘ruf ibn Kharbudh, Salam al-Ju‘fi, al-Qasim ibn Sulayman, Yunus ibn ‘Abd al-Rahman al-Ashal, and ‘Abd al-Rahim al-Qasir from Abu Ja‘far and Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon them). It was also narrated by al-Qummi in his *Tafsir* as a suspended (مضمر) report, and by al-‘Ayyashi from Abu al-Tufayl from ‘Ali (peace be upon him).

In some of these narrations, others are mentioned alongside the Umayyads, and what was previously discussed regarding the meaning of the verse guides toward this. Narrations have also passed under His saying: **وَمَثَلُ كَلِمَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ كَشَجَرَةٍ خَبِيثَةٍ** "And the parable of an evil word is that of an evil tree..." (14:26), stating that the evil tree refers to the two most wicked clans of the Quraysh.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, ‘Abd al-Razzaq, Sa‘id ibn Mansur, Ahmad, al-Bukhari, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa’i, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani, al-Hakim, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Dala’il* narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: **وَمَا جَعَلْنَا الرُّؤْيَا الَّتِي أَرَيْنَاكَ إِلَّا فِتْنَةً لِلنَّاسِ** that he said: "It was a vision of the eye (رؤيا عين) shown to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) on the night he was taken on the night journey to Jerusalem, and it was not a dream in sleep." Regarding "the cursed tree in the Qur'an," he said: "It is the tree of Zaqqum."

**I say:** This meaning is also narrated from Ibn Sa‘d, Abu Ya‘la, and Ibn ‘Asakir from Umm Hani. You have already seen the status of this narration in the discussion on the commentary of the verse.

In the same source, Ibn Jarir and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding His saying: **وَمَا جَعَلْنَا الرُّؤْيَا الَّتِي أَرَيْنَاكَ** that he said: "The Messenger of Allah was shown that he and his companions entered Mecca while he was in Medina at that time. So he traveled to Mecca before the appointed time, but the polytheists turned him back. Some people said: 'He has been turned back, yet he had told us that he would enter it.' So his return was their trial (فتنة)."

**I say:** What applies to this narration has already been mentioned in the commentary of the verse, in addition to the fact that it contradicts what preceded it.

In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from Husayn ibn Sa‘id in the book *al-Zuhd*, from ‘Uthman ibn ‘Isa from ‘Umar ibn Udhaynah from Sulayman ibn Qays who said: "I heard the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) saying: 'The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: Indeed, Allah has forbidden Paradise to every foul-mouthed (فحاش), obscene person of little modesty who does not care what he says or what is said to him. For if you were to investigate him, you would find him to be either a product of fornication (لغية) or a partnership of the Devil (شرك شيطان).'"

A man asked: "O Messenger of Allah, is there a partnership of the Devil among the people?" He replied: "Do you not read the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَشَارِكْهُمْ فِي الْأَمْوَالِ وَالْأَوْلَادِ** (and share with them in wealth and children)?"

He asked: "Who is the one who does not care what he says or what is said to him?" He replied: "Yes, the one who attacks people and speaks about them while knowing that they will not leave him alone; that is the one who does not care what he says or what is said to him."

In *Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi*, from Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him): "I asked him about the partnership of the Devil (شرك الشيطان) in His saying: **وَشَارِكْهُمْ فِي الْأَمْوَالِ وَالْأَوْلَادِ**. He said: 'Whatever wealth is forbidden (حرام) is a partnership of the Devil.' He said: 'And he is with the man until he has intercourse, so it [the child] is from his sperm and the man's sperm if it was forbidden.'"

**I say:** The narrations regarding these meanings are numerous, and they are of the nature of mentioning specific instances (المصاديق). The comprehensive meaning for them has already been presented.

What is mentioned in them regarding his partnership with the man in intercourse, the sperm, and so on, is a metaphor (كناية) for his having a portion in all of that. It is a form of representation (التمثيل) by which the intended meaning is clarified, and its parallels are many in the narrations.
